summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the PC industry fundamentally lacks design leadership, using Steve Jobs' 1995 criticism of Microsoft as a launching point. He contends that while Apple integrates taste and culture into products from inception, PC manufacturers treat design as an afterthought or ignore it entirely. Atwood illustrates this with examples of ugly PC cases, poorly designed software utilities, and Microsoft's own inconsistent design guidelines. He concludes that with Apple now competing in the x86 market, the PC industry must urgently develop design competence or face irrelevance.
key_points: Apple's competitive advantage stems from treating design as integral to product development, not as an afterthought
The PC hardware industry cannot even successfully copy Apple's designs, producing aesthetically inferior imitations
PC software vendors oscillate between garish custom interfaces and bland utilitarian designs, with no middle ground
Microsoft is the only company positioned to set PC design standards but fails to lead or even follow its own guidelines consistently
Jobs' vision that art and engineering are inseparable has remained consistent for decades and represents a genuine competitive threat to the PC industry
money_quotes: At Apple, taste and culture are designed into every product from day one.
In the PC world, taste and culture are rarely considered, and if they are, it's always as an afterthought.
There are no design leaders in the PC world.
If you want a PC that looks as good as it works, you have to make it yourself.
The design of a product, the art of it, is just as important as the engineering.
People put a lot of love into these products, and a lot of expression of their appreciation came to these things.
Why choose a beige box and a schizophrenic UI when you could have something that's beautiful and thoughtfully designed for about the same price?
key_insight: Design leadership requires treating aesthetics and user experience as first-class engineering concerns from the start, not cosmetic additions bolted on after functionality is complete.
